content: I want to make a really good spaghetti carbonara tonight. What's the secret to getting the sauce silky instead of scrambled?

The trick is temperature control. Whisk the eggs and grated Pecorino together off the heat, then toss them with the hot pasta away from the burner, adding a splash of starchy pasta water to loosen it. The residual heat cooks the eggs into a creamy sauce without scrambling them.
Should I use guanciale or pancetta, and do I need cream?

You're making a classic Italian dish, I presume? Both guanciale and pancetta are excellent choices, but they have slightly different flavor profiles. Guanciale has a more delicate, unsmoked flavor, while pancetta is often smoked and has a bolder taste. If you want a more authentic carbonara experience, guanciale might be the way to go. But if you can't find guanciale or prefer a stronger flavor, pancetta is a great substitute.

As for the cream, traditional carbonara doesn't actually contain any cream. The creaminess comes from the eggs, cheese, and pasta water. Adding cream can make the dish richer, but it can also make it feel heavier and less authentic. If you want to stay true to the original recipe, you can omit the cream and focus on getting the egg and cheese mixture just right. That being said, if you're looking for a creamier sauce, a small amount of cream or even half-and-half can be added to enhance the texture. What's your personal preference?

content:
Using guanciale will definitely give your dish a more authentic flavor profile. And combining Pecorino Romano with Parmigiano-Reggiano is a great choice, as the sharpness of the Pecorino will complement the nuttiness of the Parmigiano nicely.

To emulsify the egg and cheese mixture without scrambling the eggs, I'd recommend tempering the eggs before adding the hot pasta. You can do this by slowly pouring a small amount of the hot pasta water into the egg mixture while whisking constantly. This will warm the eggs up and prevent them from scrambling when you add the hot pasta.

Another tip is to add the pasta to the egg mixture, rather than the other way around. So, you'd add the cooked spaghetti to the bowl with the egg and cheese mixture, and then toss everything together quickly. This will help distribute the heat evenly and prevent the eggs from cooking too quickly.

Finally, make sure to use a large enough bowl to hold all the ingredients, and to toss the pasta and egg mixture together quickly and confidently. You want to create a smooth, creamy sauce that coats the pasta evenly, so don't be afraid to get in there and really mix everything together. With a little practice, you should be able to achieve a beautifully emulsified carbonara sauce!
